1: The four-descriptor portrait of Job — tam (blameless/complete), yashar (upright/straight), yare’ ’elohim (fearing God), and sar mera’ (turning from evil) — forms a merism of moral completeness. Tam is the same root used for the unblemished sacrificial animal (Leviticus 1:3, tamim); it means without spot or defect, complete. Yashar = morally straight, aligned with the right. The four terms together are the OT’s most comprehensive moral character description applied to a human, echoed only in YHWH’s own repeated endorsement of Job (v8; Job 2:3).
2: The seven sons and three daughters, seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred donkeys — the numbers are schematic for totality (seven = complete, the totals doubly round). The three-seven structure of Job’s possessions and children appears at the book’s end (Job 42:12–13) but doubled, marking the restoration as exceeding the original in every category except the children, who are replaced in equal number (not doubled) — a structural detail the narrator maintains precisely.
3: Job is described as the greatest (gadol) among all bene qedem (sons/people of the East). The ‘East’ (qedem) is the region associated with wisdom traditions in the OT (1 Kings 4:30; Isaiah 2:6). Job’s identification as an easterner places him in the wisdom tradition, consistent with his dialogues’ high poetic and philosophical register. His non-Israelite identity (Uz is likely in Edom or trans-Jordan) is significant: the book makes its most acute theological argument through a figure outside the covenant community.
4: The sons’ feasts (mishteh, drinking-feast) each on his yom (appointed day) — the seven sons in rotation create a cycle of seven feasts. The phrase yom hullado (day of his birth) in 3:1 and 3 uses the same yom vocabulary. The cyclical festivity of the sons will be shattered by the wind that strikes on one of these feast days (v18–19).
5: Viqaddishem (he consecrated/sanctified them) — Job’s regular priestly intercession for his children before dawn. The verb qadash in the pi’el = cause to be holy, purify. Job functions as a priestly intercessor: he offers burnt offerings (‘olot) for each child. The concern: perhaps they ber’u ’elohim (cursed/blessed God) bilbavam (in their hearts). Note: berakh here is the first use of the curse-euphemism in the book (cf. v11, Job 2:5, Job 2:9) — the same word as “bless,” used to avoid writing the actual curse.
6: Ha-Satan — with the definite article ha, making it a title or role rather than a personal name: the adversary/accuser. Satan is from the root satan = to accuse, be hostile to, obstruct. In the legal context of the heavenly court (bene ha-’elohim presenting before YHWH), the adversary functions as a prosecuting attorney who challenges YHWH’s assessment of Job. The bene ha-’elohim (sons of God) is the divine council, not a reference to angelic beings in the later Christian sense but to the heavenly court of YHWH’s royal household (cf. Ps 82; 1 Kings 22:19-22).
7: Missut ba-’arets umitthallek bah (from roaming through the earth and walking back and forth on it) — the adversary’s self-description. The sut (roam, wander) and halak (walk) describe ceaseless investigative movement. The same vocabulary appears in Zechariah 1:10–11 and 6:7 for the angelic patrol of the earth — the divine intelligence network. The adversary has been on patrol and has evidently been observing Job.
8: YHWH initiates: hasamta libbeka ’al ’abdi ’iyyov (have you set your heart on/taken notice of my servant Job?) — the verb sam leb = to fix attention on, observe carefully. YHWH initiates the Job discussion, not the adversary. This is a detail of the Hebrew structure that the prose preserves: the test originates in YHWH’s own move to draw the adversary’s attention to Job. The fourfold moral description of v1 is repeated verbatim, framing YHWH’s own endorsement as narrative proof of Job’s character.
9: Hinam — ‘for nothing,’ ‘freely,’ ‘without cause’ — is the adversary’s thesis word. The one Hebrew word sets the book’s central question: Does Job fear God for nothing (hinam)? The same word appears in Job 2:3 (to ruin him hinam) and Psalms 35:19 / Job 69:4 (hinam enemies). The adversary’s claim is that piety is always instrumental: no one loves God for God’s own sake; everyone has a price.
10: Vayyasek ba’adhu (you have put a protective fence around him) — suk = to hedge in, protect. The adversary sarcastically identifies YHWH’s protection as the reason for Job’s piety. Ironically, in 3:23 Job will use the same concept of being ‘fenced in’ (yasek be’ado) as a complaint against God — the same enclosure becomes oppression when viewed from within suffering.
11: Velukh — stretch out. The imperative-like ulam shelah-na (but reach out now) followed by the oath ’im-lo’ ’al-panekha yevarekheka (he will surely curse you to your face) — the ’im-lo’ oath formula = “truly / if not.” The yevarakhekha = will bless/curse you. The Hebrew euphemism: the scribe (or narrator) writes “bless” (barakh) where the adversary means “curse.” The phrase ’al-panekha (to your face) heightens the audacity: Job will deny God to his face, not privately.
12: YHWH’s limit: hinno veyadelkha raq ’elaw ’al-tishlah yadekha (all he has is in your power; only do not lay a hand on the man himself). The divine limit (raq = only/but) is the theological boundary that makes the test meaningful: suffering is permitted up to a defined boundary. The adversary has power only within YHWH’s granted permission — a structural feature of Job’s suffering that Job himself never learns within the book’s narrative.
13: The four messenger scenes (vv14–19) overlap in rapid sequence — each begins “while he was still speaking” (’od zu medabber zeh). The four catastrophes bracket the full range of loss: livestock (Sabeans), livestock and servants (fire from heaven), livestock and servants (Chaldeans), and finally children. The overlapping messengers create the Hebrew narrative’s most concentrated portrait of simultaneous catastrophe.
14: The messenger (mal’ak) arrives at a run (vayarots). The oxen (baqqar, cattle for plowing) and donkeys grazing beside them — the work-animals at their tasks. The placement of the animals in normal agricultural activity highlights the ordinariness of the moment of catastrophe.
15: Vatipol Sheba (the Sabeans fell upon them) — the verb naphal = fell/attacked. The Sabeans (Sheva) are associated with South Arabia or Ethiopia in OT geography. Anokhi levaddi niltatiy (I alone escaped to tell you) — the messenger formula. The phrase will be repeated by all four messengers, creating a liturgical refrain of total destruction.
16: ’Esh ’elohim (fire of God/divine fire) fell from the heavens — lightning or what later readers might call divine fire. The destruction of the sheep and their servants by divine fire from heaven is particularly ironic: Job has been offering burnt offerings (’olot, literally “things that go up in smoke”) to God on behalf of his children (v5); now YHWH’s own fire descends and burns the sheep and servants. The sacrificial vocabulary and the destructive fire occupy the same semantic space.
17: Three Chaldean (kasdim) raiding parties (reshim shalosha = three heads/divisions) take the camels and kill the servants. The Chaldeans appear in the OT as the Babylonian empire’s ethnic core (Daniel 1:4; Jeremiah 5:15). Their appearance alongside the Sabeans (v15) places the story in a world of both south Arabian and Mesopotamian raiders — geographically broad, spanning the known world.
18: The fourth and final messenger: Job’s sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine at the oldest brother’s house — the feasting scene from v4 now given as the setting for the final catastrophe. The detail that they were at the bekhor’s (firstborn’s) house may be deliberate: the day of the firstborn, the eldest sibling’s feast, is the day of the wind.
19: Ruah gedola (a great wind) from across the desert (midbar) strikes the four corners of the house (’arba’ pinnot habayit). The “four corners” detail implies the wind surrounds and collapses the house entirely, not just strikes one side. Vayyamotu (and they died) — all the young people. The catastrophe is total: all ten children in a single event.
20: Job’s five-verb response: wayyaqom (he arose), wayyiqra’ et-me’ilo (he tore his outer garment), wayyigallach et-rosho (he shaved his head), wayyippol ’artsah (he fell to the ground), wayyishtahu (he bowed/worshipped). The first three are acts of mourning (tearing garments and shaving the head are standard mourning rituals, Isaiah 15:2; Ezra 9:3); the last two are acts of worship. The sequence is crucial: Job moves from mourning to worship in a single continuous gesture — without any reported pause or struggle.
21: The vocabulary of Job’s declaration: ’erom (naked) twice; mibeten ’immi (from my mother’s womb) / shamah ’ashub (there I shall return) — the earth as mother womb imagery. Most significantly: YHWH natan v-YHWH laqah (YHWH gave and YHWH has taken) — both the giving and the taking attributed to YHWH directly, not to the adversary. Job never learns about the heavenly court scene; his attribution of both blessings and disasters to YHWH is theologically correct but based on no inside information. The verse ends: yehi shem YHWH mevorakh (blessed/praised be the name of YHWH) — using barakh in its positive sense, the exact opposite of what the adversary predicted he would do with the same root.
22: Lo-hata’ (did not sin) and lo natan tifla le-’elohim (did not give/assign unsavoriness/blame to God). Tifla is a rare word (only Job 1:22; Job 24:12; Jeremiah 23:13) meaning something improper, unsavory, without taste — a word of moral and aesthetic disgust. Job did not charge God with tifla = did not find God’s action shameful or improper. This is the narrator’s own verdict: the first round of the test is complete; the adversary’s thesis is falsified.
